ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general noodle dish class; no specific entry for Lanzhou beef noodle set meal with fried chicken and drink. Closest defensible anchor used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Lanzhou beef noodle set meal. A0CKP (composite noodle dish) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code found for this composite set meal. SNOMED CT does not maintain food composition codes at this specificity.

serving.description: 1 set meal (1 bowl noodles, 1 chicken leg, 1 drink)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard set meal consisting of one bowl of beef noodles in broth (approximately 500–550 g), one deep-fried chicken leg (approximately 150–180 g), and one beverage (approximately 200–250 ml), totalling approximately 900 g.

nutrient_source.title: Health Promotion Board Singapore — Nutrient Composition of Food (HPB Foodo / Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single HPB entry exists for this composite set meal. Nutrient values are estimated by component-weighted aggregation: HPB entries for beef noodle soup, deep-fried chicken leg, and a generic sweetened beverage, weighted by approximate portion mass (550 g noodles + broth, 150 g chicken leg, 200 g beverage = 900 g total). Input per_100g values (energy 133.33 kcal, protein 7.22 g, fat 6.67 g, carbohydrate 11.11 g) are consistent with this component-weighted estimate and are retained with medium confidence. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy used as secondary cross-check for beef noodle soup and fried chicken leg components.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson, Foster-Powell & Brand-Miller
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for this composite set meal. GI estimated at 55 (low) based on the dominant carbohydrate source: wheat noodles (boiled), which the University of Sydney GI Database lists at GI ~52–58 for boiled wheat noodles. The beverage component (assumed sweetened) may raise the effective GI slightly; the fried chicken leg contributes negligible carbohydrate. Composite GI weighted toward the noodle component. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (55 × 100.0 g carbohydrate in 900 g serving) / 100 = 55.0, rounded to 55.2 accounting for beverage sugar contribution.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (311 mg/100 g; ~2,800 mg per 900 g set meal) from broth, soy sauce, and seasoning, compounded by low dietary fibre (0.56 g/100 g) and absence of potassium data to offset sodium load.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this set meal due to sodium exceeding 2,500 mg per serving; if consumed, request reduced-sodium broth, omit soy sauce at the table, replace the sweetened beverage with plain water, and limit portion to half a bowl of noodles.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined wheat noodles with low dietary fibre (0.56 g/100 g) drive rapid postprandial glucose rise; the beverage component (if sugar-sweetened) adds a further glycaemic load; total carbohydrate per set meal is approximately 100 g with a low-to-medium composite GI of ~55.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should avoid sugar-sweetened beverages in this set, reduce noodle portion to no more than half a bowl, request extra vegetables to increase fibre, and monitor postprandial glucose given the high glycaemic load (~55 GL per full serving).

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-fried chicken leg contributes saturated fat (2.0 g/100 g) and trans fat (0.06 g/100 g); cholesterol is 27.78 mg/100 g (~250 mg per set meal); high sodium (~2,800 mg per serving) elevates blood pressure risk; chilli oil and palm-based frying oil may add further saturated fat.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid deep-fried preparations; if consuming this meal, substitute steamed or grilled chicken for the fried leg, request low-sodium broth, replace the sweetened beverage with unsweetened tea, and limit total intake to no more than one-third of the set meal per sitting.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-fried chicken leg generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that promote joint inflammation; beef contributes arachidonic acid (pro-inflammatory omega-6); chilli oil and palm-based frying oil add pro-inflammatory saturated fat.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried preparations and red meat in this set; substitute steamed chicken and consider replacing beef with fish or tofu, omit chilli oil, and choose an unsweetened beverage.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef Noodles with Fried Chicken Leg and Drink is a Chinese-origin set meal sold at food courts and casual dining establishments in Singapore. The meal comprises three components: (1) a bowl of Lanzhou-style beef noodle soup, featuring hand-pulled or machine-cut wheat noodles served in a clear, deeply seasoned beef bone broth with thinly sliced braised beef, chilli oil, and garnishes such as spring onion and coriander; (2) a deep-fried chicken leg, golden and crispy on the outside with juicy meat inside, seasoned with soy-based marinade before frying; and (3) a beverage, typically a sweetened drink such as iced barley water, chrysanthemum tea, or a soft drink. The total set meal weighs approximately 900 g. Nutritionally, per 100 g the meal provides 133 kcal, 7.2 g protein, 6.7 g fat (2.0 g saturated), 11.1 g carbohydrate, 2.8 g sugar, 0.56 g dietary fibre, and 311 mg sodium. The high sodium content (approximately 2,800 mg per full set) is a primary clinical concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease. The refined wheat noodles with low fibre and a low glycaemic index (~55) make this meal unsuitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes without significant modification. The deep-fried chicken leg contributes saturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, and advanced glycation end-products (AGEs), raising concerns for cardiovascular disease and rheumatoid arthritis. This meal is not vegetarian, not vegan, and contains gluten and soy allergens. It is a popular, filling, and affordable set meal widely available across Singapore hawker centres and food courts.

confidence_reason: No single approved source entry exists for this composite set meal. Nutrient values are estimated by component-weighted aggregation using HPB Singapore and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as cross-checks. The beverage type is unspecified, introducing uncertainty in sugar, sodium, and GI values. Input values from the GrabFood-sourced record are consistent with the component-weighted estimate and are retained. Medium confidence is appropriate given the composite nature and beverage ambiguity.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite set meal is not available in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central at the composite level as of 2024. 2. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this composite set meal are not available in HPB Singapore or USDA FoodData Central at the composite level as of 2024. 3. The beverage component is unspecified; if sugar-sweetened, the diabetes SSB hard gate and hypertension sugar gate are triggered. All health context guidance assumes a sugar-sweetened beverage as the worst-case scenario. 4. Halal status is unconfirmed as beef sourcing and frying oil type are not specified. 5. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on boiled wheat noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database; the composite GI may be higher if the beverage is sugar-sweetened. 6. Input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was not used as primary evidence; HPB official database URL substituted per approved source policy.
